page.title: Funerary practices of the Yayoi period

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:弥生時代の墓制]]
{{ill|Yayoi era tomb system|lt=Yayoi era tomb|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi era tomb system|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} was the tomb system of the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}}. It is associated with the [[Kofun period]] where {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} became a major burial system and social organization more centralized {{Multiple issues | More footnotes = May 2024 | No footnotes = May 2024 }}
This article provides an in-depth discussion of the '''Burial practices of the Yayoi period''' (弥生時代の墓制).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], burial near dwellings was common, with communal cemeteries such as [[:en:stone circles|stone circles]] being notable. However, in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}}, communal cemeteries near settlements became the norm. Burial practices evolved from simple '''{{ill|Pit graves|ja|土壙墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit graves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 土坑墓}}''' to include the use of burial containers such as [[jar coffins]], [[Sarcophaguss]], and [[Wooden Coffin|Wooden coffins]]. Yayoi burial practices varied significantly by region and time, with differences reflecting social hierarchies.
The Yayoi burial practices can be understood in three stages: 1. **Communal graves and cemeteries**: Initially, burial was organized communally. 2. **Emergence of inequality**: Differences began to appear within communal graves. 3. **Individual or group-specific burial areas**: Specific graves or burial precincts were demarcated, often rectangular. These stages represent phases, not chronological periods, and could coexist.<ref>Yoshio Kondo, "The Formation and Transition of Keyhole-shaped Tombs," *Archaeological Studies* 15-1, 1968.</ref>
== Jar Coffin Burials == {{main|Jar burial}} '''{{ill|Jar burial|en|Jar burial|qid=Q4244233|lt=Jar burials|1=Jar burial}}''' (かめかんぼ) used [[:en:jars|jars]] and [[:en:urns|urns]] as coffins and were prominent in early to middleYayoi periods in northern [[Kyushu]].
While small jar coffins for infants existed in the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], Yayoi jar coffins were larger and used for adults. Initially, they were buried beneath {{ill|Dolmen|en|Dolmen|qid=Q101659|lt=Dolmens|1=Dolmen}}. These burials peaked in the middleYayoi period, particularly in areas like {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima, Fukuoka|qid=Q1011145|lt=Itoshima|1=Itoshima}}, {{ill|Fukuoka|en|Fukuoka|qid=Q26600|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka}}, and {{ill|Kanzaki District, Saga|en|Kanzaki District, Saga|qid=Q1043882|lt=Kanzaki District, Saga|1=Kanzaki District, Saga}}. By the late Yayoi period, jar coffin burials declined significantly. This shift reflects major social transformations.
Yayoi jar coffins were technically advanced, crafted from clay and fired under controlled conditions. Inside, bodies were often buried in a crouched position ({{ill|flexed burial|ja|屈葬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flexed burial|qq=}}), possibly to confine the spirit of the deceased. Social stratification is evident, with some coffins accompanied by grave goods, while others were not.
== Dolmen Burials == {{main|Dolmen}}
'''Dolmen burials''' (支石墓) consisted of rectangular capstones supported by several upright stones.
In Japan, dolmen burials appeared in the late Jomon period in northwestern Kyushu, influenced by the flourishing dolmen culture of southern {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}. These burials spread to regions such as {{ill|Matsura Peninsula|ja|松浦半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsura Peninsula|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Itoshima Peninsula|ja|糸島半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itoshima Peninsula|1=Itoshima Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8697965}}, and {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|en|Shimabara Peninsula|qid=Q522605|lt=Shimabara Peninsula|1=Shimabara Peninsula}}.
Early examples, such as the {{ill|Hayamashiri Dolmen|ja|葉山尻支石墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayamashiri Dolmen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Karatsu, Saga|en|Karatsu, Saga|qid=Q738275|lt=Karatsu|1=Karatsu, Saga}}, date to the early Yayoi period and contain artifacts like stone arrowheads. Some scholars suggest that dolmen burials reflect the presence of immigrants from the Korean Peninsula.
Dolmen burials declined by the early Yayoi period, but their influence persisted in limited areas, such as the {{ill|Gotō Islands|en|Gotō Islands|qid=Q1333790|lt=Goto Islands|1=Gotō Islands}} and parts of {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|qid=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}}.
== Stone Coffin Burials == '''Sarcophagus burials''' (石棺墓) used box-like coffins made of stone slabs. These burials, also known as {{ill|Box-type Sarcophagus burials|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-type Sarcophagus|墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-type Sarcophagus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-type Sarcophagus|qq=}}, appeared in the early Yayoi period, often alongside dolmens. They spread from northern Kyushu to the western parts of the {{ill|Chūgoku region|en|Chūgoku region|qid=Q127864|lt=Chugoku region|1=Chūgoku region}}, particularly along coastal areas.
== Wooden Coffin Burials == '''Wooden coffin burials''' (木棺墓) utilized coffins made of wood. Initially common in northern Kyushu, wooden coffins later gave way to jar coffins in communal cemeteries. In regions like {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kinki|1=Kansai region}} and the {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|qid=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay|1=Ise Bay}} area, wooden coffins remained prevalent, often in {{ill|Rectangular encircling ditches|ja|方形周溝墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular encircling ditches|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11487466}}.
== Mounded Tombs == {{main|{{ill|Yayoi mounded tombs|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}}} '''Mounded tombs''' (墳丘墓) featured raised earth mounds and appeared in the early Yayoi period. These tombs grew larger in the late Yayoi period, paving the way for the development of {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}}.
=== Rectangular Encircling Ditches (Rectangular Low Mounded Tombs) ===
{{ill|Rectangular encircling ditches|ja|方形周溝墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular encircling ditches|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11487466}} first appeared in the middleof the early {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} and reached the {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|qid=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay|1=Ise Bay}} area by the end of the early period. They later spread to southern {{ill|Kanto|en|Kanto|qid=Q3812869|lt=Kanto|1=Kanto}} by the middleof the middleYayoi period and to northern Kanto and southern [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] by the late Yayoi period.
In the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]], burial sites using wooden coffins were surrounded by rectangular ditches, 6 to 25 meters on each side, with the soil piled up to form mounds. These were often located near settlements on flat hilltops or slightly elevated alluvial areas and are called rectangular encircling ditches (方形周溝墓, ''hōkei shūkōbo''). The surfaces of these mounds show little diversity or stone arrangements. Offerings found in these tombs typically include jars, tall cups, pedestals, urns, bowls, and other pottery, with regional variations.
The term "rectangular encircling ditch" was first introduced in academic reports in {{ill|1964|en|1964|qid=Q2652|lt=1964|1=1964}} by [[Iwao Oba]], who excavated {{ill|Utsuki Mukohara Site|ja|宇津木向原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsuki Mukohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Tokyo. Previously, similar features had been identified but were not well understood. Recently, the term "rectangular low mounded tombs" (方形低墳丘墓, ''hōkei teifunkyūbo'') has also gained popularity. Additionally, some researchers now categorize rectangular encircling ditches as early [[Kofun]] tombs or early clustered tombs (群集墳, ''gunshūfun'') based on advancements in pottery chronology. These researchers often refer to them simply as "rectangular tombs" (方形墳, ''hōkeifun'').
Rectangular encircling ditches were not exclusively individual graves but often contained multiple burials, suggesting they were family tombs. However, differences in grave goods, the use of red pigments, and other factors indicate a level of social stratification. These tombs were often clustered closely together, and some burials were even placed within the ditches themselves.
In the middleYayoi period, rectangular platform tombs (方形台状墓, ''hōkei daijōbo'') emerged, constructed by cutting into the surrounding earth on hills and ridges. These spread to the [[:en:Chubu region|Chubu region]] and [[:en:Kanto region|Kanto region]]. In early Yayoi Chubu and Kanto, secondary burials were common, where bodies were initially interred in pits, allowed to decompose, and then reburied in small jars or urns. As rectangular encircling ditches spread, they became the dominant burial style.
Interestingly, rectangular encircling ditches predate the Yayoi period and are found in large numbers on the Korean Peninsula. Like dolmens, this burial style is absent in China, suggesting it was introduced from southern Korea. This supports the theory that Yayoi people migrated from the Korean Peninsula rather than the Chinese Yangtze region.<ref>Kaika Kazuhiro, "The Evolution of Yayoi Burial Practices."</ref> In northern Kyushu, rectangular encircling ditches are rare, appearing primarily in the Kinki region. This scarcity is believed to reflect the rapid migration of rectangular encircling ditch-building groups from northern Kyushu to Kinki. An example from the early Yayoi period is found at the {{ill|Higashi-Odamine Site|ja|東小田峰遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Odamine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Fukuoka Prefecture. These rectangular low mounded tombs grew larger in the late Yayoi period, eventually evolving into {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}.
== Large Mounded Tombs == In the late Yayoi period, large mounded tombs emerged in regions like {{ill|Kibi|en|Kibi|qid=Q404065|lt=Kibi Province|1=Kibi}}, with notable examples such as the {{ill|Tatezuka Mounded Tomb|ja|楯築墳丘墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatezuka Mounded Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11541408}}, spanning 80 meters in length. These tombs showcased advanced construction techniques and are seen as precursors to the keyhole-shaped tombs (前方後円墳) of the Kofun period.
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Pit-type graves|ja|土坑墓|1=Pit graves|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit-type graves|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit graves|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
